Financing the Green Guardians
of Indonesia's coasts

A deep dive into the public, private, and market-based mechanisms that sustain the world's largest mangrove ecosystem.

Scroll to explore

  • Market-Based Mechanisms Ecotourism & Payment for Ecosystem Services Eco-certification & Sustainable Products Carbon Markets
01

The Public Sector

Government Mechanisms for Mangrove Financing

Overview

How governments fund Indonesia's mangrove future

Public sector approaches involve funding and policy support from government entities at the local, national, or international levels. These mechanisms form the backbone of Indonesia's ambitious restoration commitments, channeling resources from national budgets through technical agencies to the forest floor itself.

Three primary mechanisms structure this public financing landscape: direct government funding through domestic budgets, revenue instruments such as taxes and levies, and intergovernmental or multilateral funding through international development banks and global environmental facilities.

  • Budget: government funding channelled through Watershed Management Unit (BPDAS)
  • Mandate: BRGM mandate expanded from peat to mangroves in 2021
  • Financing: Bali tourism levy as a novel conservation revenue model
  • Partners: GEF, World Bank & UNDP among key international financiers
  • Results: National Blue Carbon Center established to coordinate stakeholders

A. Government Funding & Domestic Budgets

Direct allocation of national funds to provincial watershed management units — the front line of mangrove restoration in Indonesia.

Ministry of Forestry → BPDAS

The watershed bridge: BPDAS and National Forest Policy

We identified three channels for channelling funds from the national government to provincial governments for mangrove ecosystem conservation. First, fiscal transfers from the national government (APBN) to the provincial level can be used for mangrove conservation if the relevant activities are included in the provincial budget. The extent to which conservation is prioritized depends on the region’s commitment as outlined in its development plans.

Second, the national government allocates a technical deconcentration budget to the Watershed Management Unit (Balai Pengelolaan Daerah Aliran Sungai, or BPDAS), a technical unit within the Ministry of Finance (MoF), for rehabilitation purposes. BPDAS serves as a critical bridge between national policy and localized watershed management.

"The BPDAS plays a key role implementing mangrove conservation efforts by coordinating restoration activities, managing budgets, and monitoring progress — acting as a critical bridge between national forestry policy and localized watershed management interventions."

Based on the Ditjen PDASRH report, BPDAS across Indonesia restored 1,878 hectares (ha) of mangroves during 2022 to 2023.

1,878Hectares restored by BPDAS (2022–2023)
41,812Hectares planted by BRGM (2021–2023)
31Provinces covered by BRGM planting
📊 BPDAS Mangrove Rehabilitation MapOpen full screen ↗
Figure 1. Total planted mangrove across Indonesia facilitated by BPDAS during 2022–2023Source: Annual performance report of Ditjen PDASRH for 2022–2023

Third, the Indonesia Peat and Mangrove Restoration Agency (BRGM) also managed mangrove rehabilitation. Unlike BPDAS, whose funds are channelled through the MoF, BRGM receives direct allocations from the national budget. BRGM is also entitled to receive international grants, and funding from partners and the Environmental Fund Management Agency (BPDLH). BRGM was originally established in 2016 as the Peatland Restoration Agency (BRG). In 2021, BRG’s mandate expanded to include mangrove restoration, prompting its renaming to BRGM. From 2021 to 2023, 41,812 ha of mangrove forests were planted across 31 provinces. Most rehabilitation efforts took place in North Sumatra, Riau and East Kalimantan provinces. BRGM is now inactive – its duties were transferred to Ditjen PDASRH through its Directorate of Inland Water and Mangrove Rehabilitation (RPDM). .

  • Origin: founded 2016 as BRG (peatland only)
  • Reconfiguration: renamed BRGM in 2021 (additional mangrove mandate)
  • Outcomes: 41,812 ha planted across 31 provinces (2021–2023)
  • Key regions: North Sumatra, Riau, East Kalimantan
  • Status: inactive – transferred to Ditjen PDASRH / RPDM
📊 BRGM Planted Mangrove MapOpen full screen ↗
Figure 2. Total planted mangrove across Indonesia facilitated by BRGM during 2021–2023Source: Peatland and Mangrove Restoration Agency Performance Report 2021–2023
🏛️

B. Taxes, Levies & Surcharges

Revenue instruments designated for conservation — an emerging frontier for sustainable coastal financing in Indonesia.

Fiscal Instruments

Bali's Tourism Levy: A Blueprint for Blue Finance?

Indonesia has not yet explicitly designated revenue from taxes, levies and surcharges for mangrove management. The only potential connection to mangrove management is the tourism tax applied in Bali, under Governor Regulation No. 36/2023. Introduced in 2024, the Bali tourism tax is a mandatory one-time levy of IDR 150,000 charged to international visitors entering Bali for tourism.

While the regulation mentions that funds will support environmental preservation, there is no specific allocation for mangrove or blue economy initiatives. Nevertheless, this scheme demonstrates potential for future integration with mangrove-related programmes. It could serve as a model for other provinces seeking financial mechanisms for coastal conservation..

  • Governor Regulation No. 36/2023
  • IDR 150,000 per international visitor (one-time)
  • Launched in 2024
  • Supports environmental preservation & cultural heritage
  • No specific mangrove allocation yet
  • Potential blueprint for other coastal provinces
🌐

C. Intergovernmental Funding & Public Development Banks

International organizations as catalysts — grants, loans, and results-based financing for Indonesia's blue forests.

International Finance

Global Partners for local mangroves

International organizations provide financial support through grants, loans, concessional financing and results-based payments. These are often complemented by technical assistance in carbon accounting, methodology development, monitoring and community capacity strengthening. We identified the World Bank, Global Environment Facility, United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), Japan-ASEAN Integration Fund and Green Climate Fund as primary international contributors.

GEF Blue Forests
Establishing a National Blue Carbon Center through a four-year, ~$1.86M initiative across 100,000 ha in Tomini Bay & Derawan Islands
More info ↗
ATSEA Phase II
Strengthening regional governance and integrated coastal management across productive marine areas in the Arafura & Timor Seas
More info ↗
M4CR — World Bank
Rehabilitating extensive mangrove habitats in Riau, North Sumatra, North Kalimantan and East Kalimantan
More info ↗
JAIF Mangrove
Developing regional guidelines, information systems and community empowerment strategies as part of a mangrove management project
More info ↗
UNDP Adaptation Fund
Advancing coastal resilience through silvofishery, climate risk mapping and early warning systems with inclusive community training.
More info ↗
🤝

D. Bilateral Donors

Country-to-country partnerships spanning research, community empowerment, climate resilience, and results-based financing.

Bilateral Partnerships

Indonesia's global conservation alliances

Indonesia has actively pursued multiple bilateral partnerships to strengthen mangrove conservation, restoration and sustainable management. It has agreements with Singapore, the United Kingdom, the United Arab Emirates, the United States, China, Norway and Australia, among others

Indonesia – SingaporeLeadership continuity in bilateral ties

Singapore PM Lee Hsien Loong and Indonesian President Joko Widodo met for their seventh annual leaders' retreat, reaffirming commitment to continuity in bilateral ties and environmental cooperation.

More Information ↗
Indonesia – United KingdomBlue Planet Fund — £500M marine protection

The Blue Planet Fund is the UK's £500 million programme supporting developing countries to protect the marine environment and reduce poverty, with approximately 3 billion people relying on the ocean for food security.

More Information ↗
Indonesia – UAEMohamed bin Zayed–Joko Widodo Mangrove Center

Indonesia and the UAE are collaborating to build a mangrove research center in Bali — the Mohamed bin Zayed–Joko Widodo Center — dedicated to advancing mangrove science and conservation.

More Information ↗
Indonesia – NorwayCarbon trading & mangrove ecosystem protection

Norway is strengthening its cooperation with Indonesia in the field of environment and climate, focusing on carbon trading, waste management, and mangrove ecosystem protection.

More Information ↗
Indonesia – United StatesFOLU Net Sink Bilateral Framework (USAID/KLHK)

Indonesia's Ministry of Environment and Forestry (KLHK) and USAID launched the Bilateral Framework Agreement on FOLU Net Sink, addressing climate and conservation at national scale.

More Information ↗
Indonesia – ChinaBlue Carbon Cooperation — Mangroves & Seagrass

China and Indonesia have officially strengthened strategic blue carbon cooperation, marked by a collaboration agreement between the Hainan International Blue Carbon Research Centre (HiBC) and BRIN at the 2025 Hainan Blue Carbon Forum.

More Information ↗
Indonesia – AustraliaBlue Carbon Accelerator Fund (BCAF)

The BCAF, launched by the Australian Government with IUCN, provides targeted financing for coastal blue carbon ecosystems across Misool Island, Southeast Sulawesi, Sesayap River delta, Lahewa MPAs, Central Java, and West Nusa Tenggara.

More Information ↗
💱

E. Debt-for-Nature Swaps

Innovative financial agreements turning debt obligations into conservation investments for Indonesia's coastal ecosystems.

Innovative Finance

$35 Million Debt-for-Reef: The Indonesia–US Blue Swap

Indonesia and the United States have an innovative debt-for-nature swap scheme in the blue sector. It allows the US government to forgive $35 million of Indonesia’s debt over nine years starting in 2024. While coral reefs are the primary target, the agreement also encompasses mangrove and other coastal ecosystems as part of a broader strategy to promote sustainable marine biodiversity conservation.

Indonesia commits to redirecting funds that would have been used for debt payments into a dedicated conservation fund focused on protecting coral reefs in the Bird’s Head Seascape and Lesser Sunda-Banda Seascape within the Coral Triangle.

The debt swap is facilitated under the US Tropical Forest and Coral Reef Conservation Act. It is implemented with partners like Conservation International, The Nature Conservancy, Yayasan Konservasi Alam Nusantara (YKAN) and Yayasan Konservasi Cakrawala Indonesia. Despite the funding freeze by the United States Agency for International Development, the debt–swap agreement itself remains active – it is a distinct bilateral agreement.

  • Budget: $35 million debt forgiven over nine years from 2024
  • Management: facilitated under US Tropical Forest and Coral Reef Conservation Act (TFCCA)
  • Primary focus: coral reefs in the Coral Triangle
  • Secondary focus: mangrove and coastal ecosystems
  • Key areas: Bird’s Head & Lesser Sunda-Banda Seascapes
  • Partners: CI, TNC, YKAN, Yayasan Konservasi Cakrawala Indonesia
  • Status: agreement remains active despite USAID freeze
🔀

F. Blended Finance

Merging public, private, and philanthropic capital to sustain Indonesia's marine protected areas and mangrove ecosystems.

Blended Finance Mechanisms

Community, Capital & Conservation in Concert

Blended finance mechanisms play a pivotal role in Indonesia’s mangrove and marine conservation. This role is exemplified by the Banggai Marine Protected Area (MPA) Network in Central Sulawesi, launched in 2021. Partners comprise the Central Sulawesi Provincial Government, Blue Alliance, UNDP, the Ocean Risk and Action Resilience Alliance, the International Union for Conservation of Nature and local Banggai governments. Together, the partners engage coastal communities, fishers and rangers in mangrove conservation and sustainable livelihoods. The blended finance facility merges public, private and philanthropic funds to sustain MPA operations, protecting over 856,000 ha of marine and mangrove habitats.

🐠 Banggai MPA Network — Blue AllianceOpen full screen ↗
Banggai Marine Protected Area Network — Blue Alliance, Central SulawesiSource: bluealliance.earth

Another notable example is the Biru Fund, initiated by YKAN. With support from the Global Fund for Coral Reefs (GFCR), YKAN is developing the Biru Fund. This blended finance initiative channels public, private and philanthropic resources to small and medium enterprises and coastal groups in marine conservation and the inclusive blue economy. Other key aims include capacity building; mentoring for sustainable growth, linking conservation (reefs, mangroves) to local livelihoods; and reducing risks, while attracting private capital.

🐚 Biru Fund — YKAN Blue Finance Initiative
🏗️

G. Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs)

Integrating environmental restoration with sustainable economic development across Indonesia's coastal and marine areas.

Multi-Sector Collaboration

Shrimp to seagrass: PPPs scaling coastal resilience

Our study highlights several public-private partnership (PPP) schemes in Indonesia that combine livelihood improvements with ecosystem rehabilitation, especially of mangroves, coral reefs and seagrass. The Shrimp–Carbon Aquaculture (SECURE) programmes in East Kalimantan and South Sumatra demonstrate how PPPs support blue carbon initiatives by restoring mangrove ecosystems while promoting responsible shrimp farming. Other initiatives, such as TransformaSea Gili Balu and the Sungsang Mangrove Restoration and Ecotourism (SMART) programme, showcase PPPs that emphasize conservation-driven tourism and community empowerment.

Program NameLocationProvince
Shrimp-Carbon Aquaculture (SECURE)BerauEast Kalimantan
Shrimp-Carbon Aquaculture (SECURE)Ogan Komering IlirSouth Sumatra
TransformaSea Gili BaluSumbawa BaratWest Nusa Tenggara
Sungsang Mangrove Restoration & Ecotourism (SMART)BanyuasinSouth Sumatra
📈

H. Blue Bonds

Indonesia's sovereign and samurai blue bonds — pioneering capital market instruments for sustainable marine and coastal ecosystem management.

Capital Market Instruments

Three waves of blue bond issuance

To date, the Government of Indonesia has launched three blue bonds. In 2023, it announced the first issuance of sovereign blue bonds, followed by the second and third samurai bond phases in 2024 and 2025. These actions reflect Indonesia’s strategic commitment to financing sustainable marine and coastal ecosystem management through innovative capital market instruments.

Phase I · 2023
Sovereign Blue Bond
RIJPY0530 & RIJPY0533 — Indonesia’s pioneering sovereign-level blue bond, setting a landmark for marine conservation finance in the Asia-Pacific region.
View on DJPPR ↗
Phase II · 2024
Samurai Bond Phase II
The second issuance of Japan-targeted samurai blue bonds, deepening Indonesia's access to Japanese capital markets for coastal and marine ecosystem financing.
View on DJPPR ↗
Phase III · 2025
Samurai Bond Phase III
The most recent issuance in 2025, further scaling Indonesia’s blue finance ambitions through continued engagement with Japan’s sustainable investment community.
View on DJPPR ↗
🏦

I. Trust Funds

Neutral third-party organizations pooling donor funds to ensure transparency, accountability, and alignment with national conservation priorities.

Environmental Finance Infrastructure

IELH & ICCTF: Indonesia’s trusted conservation finance intermediaries

In Indonesia, two neutral third-party organizations play a crucial role in pooling donor funds for environmental and climate-related projects: the Indonesia Environment Trust Fund (IELH/BPDLH) and the Indonesia Climate Change Trust Fund (ICCTF). These entities act as trusted intermediaries managing, allocating and overseeing financing from multiple donors. In this way, they ensure transparency, accountability and alignment with national priorities.

Mangroves for Coastal Resilience (M4CR)

This fund is managed through the Sustainable Landscape Management Multi-Donor Trust Fund (SLM-MDTF) with oversight by IELH from 2020 to 2026. It pools resources from various donors to finance integrated landscape and mangrove restoration programmes to enhance coastal resilience.

World Bank Indonesia Sustainable Landscapes ↗

COREMAP-CTI-ICCTF

This trust fund, managed by ICCTF from 2020 to 2022, supports coral reef and coastal ecosystem rehabilitation as part of the Coral Triangle Initiative. It channels donor funds to protect marine biodiversity, while integrating climate adaptation measures.

ICCTF Projects ↗

AFD within ICCTF Framework

The French Development Agency (AFD) funds this trust, managed by ICCTF, for 2022–2026. It supports Indonesia’s blue carbon strategy, focusing on mangrove conservation to boost carbon sequestration and enhance environmental resilience.

AFD Mangrove & Blue Carbon Strategy ↗    ICCTF-AFD ↗
02

The Private Sector

Corporate & Market Mechanisms for Mangrove Financing

💳

A. Microfinance & Sustainability-Linked Loans

Accessible financing for individuals and SMEs engaged in blue carbon projects, including mangrove management programs.

Community Finance

Bio-Rights: Conservation credit as community uplift

To date, we have identified only one microcredit scheme implemented specifically to sustain mangrove ecosystems: the Bio-Rights Wetland Indonesia project. Bio-Rights is an innovative financing mechanism that combines poverty alleviation with environmental conservation by providing credit to local communities. This credit is transformed into a grant once communities achieve agreed conservation outcomes, such as mangrove restoration or peatland protection.

The project is part of the broader Building with Nature Indonesia initiative, which started in 2013 with pilots in Demak on Java’s north coast and peatland areas in South Tapanuli. Project activities include mangrove rehabilitation, canal blocking, paludiculture, water-level monitoring and fire risk reduction. It involves multi-stakeholder collaboration with government agencies, non-governmental organizations (NGOs), research institutions and local communities, funded by international partners. Bio-Rights aims to create lasting ecosystem resilience and socioeconomic benefits by aligning financial incentives with environmental restoration goals.

🏢

B. Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)

How Indonesia's corporations and state-owned enterprises are funding mangrove restoration as part of their environmental stewardship commitments.

Corporate Financing

From scattered acts to programmatic investment

Corporate social responsibility (CSR) reflects companies’ obligations to affect society and the environment in a positive way. Many corporations fund mangrove restoration to mitigate their ecological footprints and enhance their public image through environmental stewardship. Based on our analysis, most CSR-funded mangrove programmes are concentrated in key coastal provinces such as DKI Jakarta, Central Java, West Java, Banten and Bali.

Institutionally, mangrove CSR financing is driven largely by state-owned enterprises (SOEs). These include Pupuk Kalimantan Timur, Pertamina, Bank Nasional Indonesia (BNI), Bank Rakyat Indonesia (BRI), Badak LNG, PLN and Telkom Indonesia. SOEs are complemented by private companies in the energy, plantation and manufacturing sectors.

CSR-financed mangrove restoration has accelerated sharply in recent years, rising from only a few cases in 2021 to a peak in 2024, aligned with the National Mangrove Rehabilitation Programme (2021–2024) and growing ESG commitments across corporate sectors..

In Banyuwangi, BNI planted 78,000 mangroves in Pangpang Bay in 2024. In the same year, SKK Migas in Banyuasin planted 31,000 mangroves in Sungsang Sub-District.

  • Key CSR provinces: DKI Jakarta, Central Java, West Java, Banten, Bali
  • SOE leaders: Pertamina, BNI, BRI, PLN, Telkom Indonesia, Badak LNG
  • BNI results: 78,000 mangroves planted in Pangpang Bay, Banyuwangi (2024)
  • SKK Migas results: 31,000 mangroves planted in Sungsang, Banyuasin (2024))
  • Scaling: growth from scattered philanthropy to programmatic ESG investment
  • Global results: millions of seedlings planted across diverse coastal landscapes
📊 CSR Mangrove Distribution MapOpen full screen ↗
Figure 3. Spatial distribution of mangrove planting activities claimed by corporate social responsibility (CSR) programs across IndonesiaSource: News scraping analysis using keywords "CSR" and "Mangrove" (2021–2025)
💚

C. Blue or Green Savings

Innovative financial products that channel individual bank savings toward mangrove conservation and restoration projects.

Sustainable Banking

Saving for the sea: Green & blue savings programmes

Green and blue savings programmes are innovative financial products that channel individual savings towards environmental initiatives. Two programs have been identified: BritAma Tanam Kebaikan and TORA Green and Blue Savings. BritAma Tanam Kebaikan is run by BRI in collaboration with BenihBaik. When customers open a BritAma savings account via BRImo with a minimum balance of IDR 1 million, BRI donates one mangrove seedling. The TORA programmes, offered by J Trust Bank, link customer savings with environmental conservation: TORA Green Savings contributes 0.5% of customers’ interest earnings to fund mangrove reforestation on Harapan Island in Kepulauan Seribu.

BritAma Tanam Kebaikan

In collaboration with Benih Baik, every customer who opens a BRI BritAma savings account through BRImo with a minimum balance of Rp 1 million is equivalent to planting a mangrove seedling. The programme integrates ESG principles in banking operations and has planted tens of thousands of mangrove trees in multiple regions.

More Information ↗

TORA Green Savings & TORA Blue Ocean Savings

J Trust Bank announced changes to its TORA Green Savings and TORA Blue Ocean Savings programmes. It introduced two dedicated savings products with clearer benefits for environmental and marine ecosystem protection. It now distributes customer donations to organizations and activities beyond the original partners.

More Information ↗
🌱

D. NGOs

Various programs related to mangrove management implemented by non-governmental organizations across Indonesia.

Civil Society Action

NGO programmes across Indonesia’s mangrove landscapes

We collected information on various programmes related to mangrove management implemented by several NGOs across Indonesia to provide examples of past and ongoing efforts. We classified these programmes into seven categories: coastal waste management; education; institutional strengthening and human resource capacity building; mangrove ecotourism; mangrove rehabilitation or restoration; regulatory strengthening; and sustainable aquaculture.

🗺️ NGO Mangrove Program DistributionOpen full screen ↗

03

Market-Based Mechanisms

Payment for Ecosystem Services, Carbon Markets & Eco-certification

🌊

A. Payment for Ecosystem Services

Mapping ecotourism sites and PES research locations across Indonesia's coastal mangrove landscapes.

Ecotourism & PES Research

141 Sites: The Spread of Mangrove Ecotourism in Indonesia

We collected data from location-based social networks through the Google Maps API to map sites focused on mangroves and ecotourism along coastal areas in Indonesia. We identified 141 ecotourism and attraction sites across Indonesia and found 12 publications related to the assessment of payment for ecosystem services for mangrove ecosystems.

🗺️ Mangrove Ecotourism & PES Sites MapOpen full screen ↗
Distribution of mangrove ecotourism sites and PES research locations in IndonesiaSource: Google Maps API data and systematic literature review
International Climate Finance

B. Article 6 of the Paris Agreement

Indonesia has established at least three key agreements under Article 6 of the Paris Agreement. The recent Verra-Indonesia Mutual Recognition Agreement (2025) is a positive milestone that allows projects registered under Verra’s Verified Carbon Standard (VCS) to simultaneously register in Indonesia's national carbon registry (SRN-PPI). This enables streamlined registration, data exchange and international carbon trading covering all VCS methodologies, including mangrove and forest conservation. Moreover, the Indonesia-Japan Mutual Recognition Arrangement (MRA), launched at COP29 as the first MRA under Article 6.2, allows Indonesia to issue carbon credits officially recognized and purchased by Japan. This targets over 200 million tons of CO2-equivalent credits annually at approximately $10 per credit. Indonesia and South Korea are also exploring cooperation in mangrove restoration and blue carbon development under an MoU on Article 6, with formal agreement pending. Together, these three agreements form a comprehensive architecture. On the one hand, they enable Indonesia to scale up nature-based climate mitigation projects, especially mangrove restoration. On the other, they mobilize international climate finance and integrate Indonesia’s national carbon market with global carbon trading frameworks. This synergy not only advances Indonesia’s Nationally Determined Contributions but also supports sustainable development and innovation in blue carbon solutions under the Paris Agreement.

🐚

C. Eco-certification & Sustainable Products

ASC certification, Aquaculture Improvement Projects, and mangrove-derived product markets across Indonesia.

Sustainable Aquaculture

Eco-certification & Sustainable Products

Eco-certification programmes such as the Aquaculture Stewardship Council (ASC) certification and Aquaculture Improvement Projects (AIP) promote more sustainable and responsible aquaculture practices in Indonesia. The following map shows the distribution of ASC-certified sites and AIP programme locations across Indonesia.

🦐 ASC & AIP Certification SitesOpen full screen ↗
Map of ASC and AIP program sites in Indonesia related to sustainable aquaculture managementLocations of Aquaculture Stewardship Council (ASC) certified sites and Aquaculture Improvement Project (AIP) sites

We also investigated the development of mangrove product-based businesses in Indonesia using their presence on major e-commerce platforms (Tokopedia and Shopee) as an indicator. Most mangrove derivative products are sold as food items, including keripik (chips), dodol (a traditional sweetner), flour and syrup. Some vendors offer mangrove-based textiles that use natural dyes extracted from mangrove leaves.

🛒 Mangrove Products Distribution MapOpen full screen ↗
Figure 6. Map distribution of mangrove-derived products available in marketplaces, categorized by product type

D. National Carbon Markets

Indonesia's regulatory framework enabling carbon trading for forestry and mangrove restoration projects.

Regulatory Framework

Indonesia's National Carbon Market: An Evolving Architecture

Indonesia’s national carbon market operates under a comprehensive regulatory framework aligned with the Paris Agreement and designed not to compromise the achievement of NDCs. Presidential Regulation No. 98/2021 established the legal basis for carbon trading, results-based payments, carbon levies and climate financing across forestry, energy, waste, industry and agriculture.

This has since been replaced by Presidential Regulation No. 110/2025, which introduces enhanced carbon trading mechanisms that distinguish between Corresponding Adjustment (CA) units – transferable internationally under Article 6 of the Paris Agreement – and non-CA units restricted to domestic markets.

A parallel development, Government Regulation No. 27/2025, formally incorporates mangroves, seagrasses and tidal wetlands into Indonesia's national carbon inventory, marking a critical step for blue carbon integration. All carbon transactions must be recorded in the SRN-PPI, now transitioning to the decentralized SRUK platform. This ensures transparency, prevents double counting and tracks NDC progress. Since implementing regulations for this new framework have not yet been published, we have not discussed this new regulation in detail here.

  • Presidential Reg. No. 98/2021 — original legal basis for carbon trading & NEK system
  • Presidential Reg. No. 110/2025 — distinguishes CA vs. non-CA units; sector-based allocation
  • Gov. Reg. No. 27/2025 – integrates mangroves, seagrasses & tidal wetlands into carbon inventory
  • MoEF Reg. No. 21/2022 — sets emission caps (PTBAE) and compliance rules
  • SRN-PPI → SRUK — national registry transitioning to decentralized platform
  • 11 accredited Validation & Verification Bodies (LVVs) for credit certification
Carbon Trading Mechanism

How the system works: caps, credits & compliance

The system allocates emission quotas to regulated business actors for defined compliance periods. Actors that reduce emissions below their caps generate surplus credits available for sale; those exceeding caps must purchase credits or offsets to comply.

Under Presidential Reg. No. 98/2021, emission limits were established through a technical approval process administered by relevant sectoral ministers. The framework applied to businesses and activities that met specific greenhouse gas (GHG) emission thresholds set by ministerial decree. When business actors’ emissions exceeded their upper GHG emission limits, they were permitted to purchase carbon units from other participants to achieve compliance. This approach provided a straightforward compliance pathway but lacked granular sectoral differentiation.

The transition to Presidential Reg. No. 110/2025 introduced a more sophisticated regulatory architecture. The new framework organizes emission trading by designated sectors and sub-sectors, with relevant ministers identifying specific “regulated installations” within their jurisdictions. Emission limits are now determined through a carbon allocation system that considers sectoral characteristics, historical emissions, reduction potential and strategic economic priorities. Businesses exceeding emission limits have three options: pay a carbon tax; purchase emission allowances from other market participants; or acquire offsets from verified projects like reforestation or renewable energy.

3Actor categories: actor categories: Surplus (A), deficit (B) & voluntary (C))
11accredited LVVs
3compliance options under Presidential Reg. No. 110/2025
Offset Mechanism

The emission cap system is set through MoEF Regulation No. 21/2022, which establishes two critical technical instruments. The PTBAE for Business Actors (PTBAE-PU) establishes the GHG emission cap for business actors and determines emission quotas within a specific compliance period for each entity. Additionally, the Technical Determination of Emission Cap (PTBAE) serves as the foundation for the relevant Minister in determining PTBAE-PU allocations.

Credits are issued as Greenhouse Gas Emission Reduction Certificates (SPE-GRK), following project registration, validation, monitoring and third-party verification by one of Indonesia's 11 accredited LVVs. Eligible methodologies must meet at least one recognized standard – those of the Director General, the National Standardization Agency (BSN) or the UNFCCC.

Figure 7. Carbon Trading Mechanism under Presidential Regulation No. 98/2021 and Ministry of Environment and Forestry Regulation No. 21/2022
Mangrove Carbon Projects in Indonesia

Six Blue Carbon Projects Driving Indonesia's Green Economy

We identified six mangrove-based carbon projects operating across Indonesia, ranging from community-led initiatives to large-scale commercial restoration. Collectively, these projects demonstrate that blue carbon finance can simultaneously restore ecosystems and generate measurable social co-benefits. These benefits include job creation, women-led restoration programmes, livelihood diversification and climate resilience for coastal communities. Most projects involve collaboration between local NGOs, international investors and advisory firms, reflecting an inclusive financing model.

Yet a consistent and significant gap persists: most of these projects lack transparent information on the carbon credit standards applied and the market prices achieved. Only two – Ibu Bakau Community and the Blue Carbon Project – explicitly reference Gold Standard certification. The rest operate under unknown or undisclosed standards, reflecting a broader immaturity in Indonesia’s carbon market. Pricing signals remain unclear and benefit-sharing with communities is inadequately governed.

Project Name Location Target Community Benefits Standard Link
Vlinder Indonesia Blue Carbon North Sumatra, Aceh 870 ha · 553,900 t removal New jobs, women-focused upskilling, community education, extreme weather protection Unknown ↗
Sumitomo & VNV Advisory Sumatra 600,000 t-CO₂ over 20 years Job opportunities, improved livelihoods, tsunami mitigation, biodiversity conservation Unknown ↗
Pesisir Biru Nusantara Indonesia-wide 30,000+ ha · 300,000+ credits/yr Sustainable & gender-equitable income, increased food security Unknown ↗
Climeco, Yakopi & PUR Projet Indonesia-wide 6,000 acres · carbon credits Improved livelihoods, community engagement, gender-equitable employment Unknown ↗
Ibu Bakau Community Aceh, N. Sumatra, Riau, Lampung, Riau Islands, W. Sumatra, Bengkulu, Jambi 85,000 tCO₂e/yr · 6.1M trees (1,672 ha) Women-led restoration, alternative livelihoods, capacity building Gold Standard ↗
Blue Carbon Project N. Sumatra, Aceh, Java 8,000 ha (Sumatra) · 25,000 ha conservation · 101,248–131,584 tCO₂e/yr · 3.0–4.9M tCO₂e over 30 yrs Improved livelihoods, women-focused, eco-tourism promotion, climate adaptation, community training Gold Standard ↗

Key strengths of the Indonesian carbon market include robust measurement, reporting and verification systems; alignment with global carbon market standards; and incentives for public-private investment. However, persistent implementation gaps remain: heavy reliance on technical regulations; administrative complexity for smaller actors; unclear long-term pricing signals; limited community benefit-sharing guidance; and risk of fragmented ministerial interpretation.

End of Story
